name: World Wonders & Landmarks
description: From the Great Pyramid to the Burj Khalifa — humanity's greatest structures and Earth's most spectacular natural formations. Learn what makes each wonder remarkable, where to find them, and the stories behind their creation.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the original height of the Great Pyramid of Giza when it was first completed?
facts.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza was originally 146.6 metres (481 feet) tall when completed around 2600 BC. Today it stands at about 138.5 metres due to the loss of its outer white limestone casing stones and the capstone.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Great Pyramid against a desert sunset, originally sheathed in gleaming white limestone

facts.quizQuestion: For which Egyptian pharaoh was the Great Pyramid of Giza built?
facts.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built as a tomb for Pharaoh Khufu (also known by the Greek name Cheops), the second pharaoh of the Fourth Dynasty. It was constructed around 2600 BC and took approximately 26 years to build.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a pharaoh in golden headdress before the Great Pyramid

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many stone blocks make up the Great Pyramid of Giza?
facts.explanation: The Great Pyramid contains approximately 2.3 million stone blocks weighing a total of around 6 million tonnes. Most blocks are local limestone, but the inner chambers used red granite quarried at Aswan — approximately 900 km away.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art close-up of massive limestone blocks stacked in precise courses

facts.quizQuestion: In what period was the Colosseum in Rome constructed?
facts.explanation: The Colosseum was built between 72 and 80 AD. Construction was begun by Emperor Vespasian of the Flavian dynasty and completed under his son Titus. It is also known as the Flavian Amphitheatre.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Colosseum at dusk with torchlight glowing from within its arched galleries

facts.quizQuestion: What was the seating capacity of the ancient Roman Colosseum?
facts.explanation: The Colosseum could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators, making it the largest amphitheatre ever built. It measured 188 metres long, 156 metres wide, and 48–50 metres high.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art aerial view of the Colosseum with a packed crowd of Roman citizens in tiered seats

facts.quizQuestion: During which period was the Parthenon on the Acropolis of Athens constructed?
facts.explanation: The Parthenon was built between 447 and 432 BC as a temple to the goddess Athena, patron of Athens. It was designed by architects Ictinus and Callicrates, with the sculptures overseen by Phidias.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Parthenon on the Acropolis at golden hour, its marble columns glowing amber

facts.quizQuestion: Which two architects designed the Parthenon on the Acropolis of Athens?
facts.explanation: The Parthenon was designed by architects Ictinus and Callicrates under the supervision of the Athenian statesman Pericles. The renowned sculptor Phidias oversaw the decorative sculptures and created the massive ivory-and-gold statue of Athena inside.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of ancient Greek architects studying clay tablets before the rising columns of the Parthenon

facts.quizQuestion: At what altitude above sea level does the Inca citadel of Machu Picchu sit?
facts.explanation: Machu Picchu is situated at 2,430 metres (7,970 feet) above sea level in the Cusco Region of Peru. Built by the Inca emperor Pachacuti around 1420–1450 AD, it was never discovered by Spanish conquistadors.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Machu Picchu's terraced ruins rising above clouds in the Andean mountains

facts.quizQuestion: Which American explorer brought Machu Picchu to international attention in 1911?
facts.explanation: Hiram Bingham III, a Yale University professor and explorer, brought Machu Picchu to international attention in 1911. Local farmers already knew of the ruins, but Bingham's expedition and subsequent National Geographic coverage introduced the site to the wider world.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a 1910s explorer in a wide-brimmed hat emerging from jungle to discover Machu Picchu's terraces

facts.quizQuestion: What nickname is given to the ancient city of Petra in Jordan, inspired by the colour of its sandstone cliffs?
facts.explanation: Petra is known as the 'Rose City' because of the distinctive pink-red colour of the sandstone from which it is carved. It was the capital of the Nabataean Kingdom from around the 2nd century BC, with the famous Al-Khazneh (Treasury) facade carved around the 1st century AD.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Petra's Al-Khazneh Treasury glowing deep rose-pink in afternoon light, framed by the narrow Siq gorge

facts.quizQuestion: How long is the Siq — the dramatic narrow gorge that serves as the main entrance to Petra?
facts.explanation: The Siq is a natural geological fault that forms the main entrance to Petra and stretches approximately 1.2 kilometres long. The gorge narrows at points to just 3 metres wide and rises to 80 metres on either side, creating a dramatic reveal when Al-Khazneh appears at the end.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art looking down the narrow Siq gorge, sheer canyon walls towering above as Al-Khazneh glows at the far end

facts.quizQuestion: What is the total surveyed length of all sections of the Great Wall of China, including all dynasties?
facts.explanation: A 2012 survey by China's State Administration of Cultural Heritage determined the total length of all Great Wall sections to be 21,196.18 kilometres. This includes walls built across multiple dynasties over 2,000 years — it is not a single continuous structure.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Great Wall of China snaking across mountain ridges into a misty horizon

facts.quizQuestion: Which Chinese dynasty built the best-preserved and most recognisable sections of the Great Wall, between 1368 and 1644?
facts.explanation: The most iconic and best-preserved sections of the Great Wall were built during the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644). While the first walls date to the 7th century BC, the Ming sections are what most visitors see today — built with fired bricks and featuring watchtowers.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Ming-era watchtower on the Great Wall, guards in armour scanning a moonlit landscape

facts.quizQuestion: From where were the bluestones of Stonehenge transported — a journey of approximately 250 kilometres?
facts.explanation: The smaller bluestones at Stonehenge, weighing up to 4 tonnes each, were transported from the Preseli Hills in Pembrokeshire, Wales — approximately 250 kilometres away. How Neolithic people moved them remains debated, with theories including rafts, sledges, and even glacial transport.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Stonehenge at dawn, the sun rising through the heel stone as mist drifts across Salisbury Plain

facts.quizQuestion: To what astronomical event is the main axis of Stonehenge precisely aligned?
facts.explanation: Stonehenge is aligned so that on the summer solstice (around 21 June), the rising sun shines directly through the main entrance, along the central axis, and illuminates the Altar Stone at the centre. The Heel Stone marks the point on the horizon where the sun rises.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the summer solstice sun rising through the Heel Stone at Stonehenge, golden rays illuminating the ancient circle

facts.quizQuestion: El Castillo pyramid at Chichen Itza has 91 steps on each of its 4 sides, plus the top platform — what total does this represent?
facts.explanation: El Castillo (the main pyramid at Chichen Itza) has 91 steps on each of its four stairways, plus the temple platform at the top — totalling 365, one for each day of the solar year. The pyramid stands about 30 metres tall with a 55.3-metre base.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of El Castillo pyramid at Chichen Itza during the equinox, a serpent shadow descending the staircase

facts.quizQuestion: In which country is the ancient Maya city of Chichen Itza located?
facts.explanation: Chichen Itza is located in the Yucatan Peninsula of Mexico. The city flourished between roughly the 9th and 12th centuries AD. It was declared one of the New Seven Wonders of the World in 2007.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of El Castillo pyramid amid lush Yucatan jungle under a blazing blue sky

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many terracotta soldier figures have been discovered in the pits near Xi'an, China?
facts.explanation: Over 8,000 terracotta soldier figures have been discovered in three burial pits near Xi'an, along with 130 chariots and 520 horses. They were buried with Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of a unified China, around 210–209 BCE.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of rows of terracotta warriors stretching to the horizon in a massive underground pit

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the Terracotta Army discovered by farmers digging a well near Xi'an, China?
facts.explanation: The Terracotta Army was discovered in March 1974 when local farmers digging a well near Xi'an stumbled upon the first pit. The site proved to be one of the greatest archaeological discoveries of the 20th century, revealing an entire buried army created to accompany Emperor Qin Shi Huang in the afterlife.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of farmers digging and discovering terracotta warrior heads emerging from the earth

facts.quizQuestion: In which modern country is the ancient Indus Valley city of Mohenjo-daro located?
facts.explanation: Mohenjo-daro is located in Sindh province, Pakistan. Built around 2500 BCE and abandoned around 1700 BCE, it was one of the largest cities of the ancient Indus Valley Civilisation with an estimated population of over 40,000. Its name means 'Mound of Dead Men' in Sindhi.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Mohenjo-daro's ancient grid streets and the Great Bath under a hazy Indus Valley sky

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately when was the ancient city of Mohenjo-daro built?
facts.explanation: Mohenjo-daro was built around 2500 BCE during the height of the Indus Valley Civilisation, making it approximately contemporary with the Great Pyramid of Giza. It was abandoned around 1700 BCE, possibly due to climate change, river shifts, or invasion.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of ancient Mohenjo-daro at its peak — terraced mud-brick buildings, busy streets, merchants trading

facts.quizQuestion: During which period was Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris originally constructed?
facts.explanation: Construction of Notre-Dame de Paris began in 1163 under Bishop Maurice de Sully and was largely complete by 1260, though refinements continued into the 14th century. It is a masterpiece of French Gothic architecture, famous for its flying buttresses and rose windows.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Notre-Dame Cathedral rising above the Seine at dusk, flying buttresses arching outward from the nave

facts.quizQuestion: On what date did fire devastate Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris, causing its spire to collapse?
facts.explanation: On 15 April 2019, a fire broke out during renovation work at Notre-Dame de Paris. The blaze destroyed the 93-metre spire and most of the roof. The cathedral reopened in December 2024 following an international restoration effort.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Notre-Dame's spire collapsing in flames against a smoky Paris night sky, crowds watching from across the Seine

facts.correctAnswer: Isidore of Miletus and Anthemius of Tralles
facts.quizQuestion: Which two Byzantine architects designed the Hagia Sophia, completed in 537 AD?
facts.explanation: Hagia Sophia was designed by mathematician-architects Isidore of Miletus and Anthemius of Tralles, commissioned by Emperor Justinian I. It was completed in 537 AD and was the world's largest interior space at the time of its construction.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Hagia Sophia's interior, massive dome floating above as golden mosaics shimmer in Byzantine lamplight

facts.quizQuestion: What is the diameter of the Hagia Sophia's famous dome?
facts.explanation: The dome of Hagia Sophia is 31.24 metres in diameter and sits 55.6 metres above the floor, supported by pendentives — a revolutionary architectural innovation that allowed a circular dome to sit atop a square base. The dome was revolutionary and influenced mosque architecture for centuries.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art view looking straight up into Hagia Sophia's luminous dome, windows ringing its base, golden mosaics below

facts.quizQuestion: Which Catalan architect took over the Sagrada Familia project in 1883 and devoted his life to it until his death in 1926?
facts.explanation: Antoni Gaudí took over the Sagrada Familia project in 1883 and dedicated the last 43 years of his life to it. At his death in 1926, less than a quarter of the church was complete. He is buried in the crypt and was beatified by the Catholic Church in 2021.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Sagrada Familia's soaring organic towers reaching toward a Barcelona sky, Gaudí's visionary silhouette in the foreground

facts.quizQuestion: How is the construction of the Sagrada Familia in Barcelona funded?
facts.explanation: The Sagrada Familia is funded entirely by private donations and admission fees — it receives no government funding. This makes it the world's largest active religious construction project funded solely through private means. Construction began in 1882 and is expected to complete in the late 2020s.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of donation boxes and visitors queuing before the Sagrada Familia's Nativity facade

facts.quizQuestion: How many years did it take to build St. Peter's Basilica in Vatican City?
facts.explanation: St. Peter's Basilica took 120 years to build, from 1506 to 1626. Multiple architects contributed over this period, including Bramante, Raphael, Michelangelo (who redesigned the dome at age 71), Antonio da Sangallo, and Carlo Maderno. Bernini later designed the famous colonnaded piazza.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of St. Peter's Basilica dome rising above the Vatican, Bernini's colonnaded piazza sweeping outward below

facts.quizQuestion: What is the interior floor area of St. Peter's Basilica — making it the largest church by interior volume in the world?
facts.explanation: St. Peter's Basilica has an interior floor area of 15,160 square metres, making it the largest church interior in the world. It is 218 metres long and its dome rises 136.6 metres above the floor. The basilica stands over the traditional site of Saint Peter's tomb.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of St. Peter's Basilica vast interior, the baldachin over the papal altar dwarfed by the towering nave

facts.quizQuestion: In which country is Borobudur, the world's largest Buddhist temple, located?
facts.explanation: Borobudur is located in Central Java, Indonesia. Built during the Sailendra Dynasty in the 8th–9th century AD, it is the world's largest Buddhist temple. It was abandoned in the 14th century as Java's population shifted to Islam and was rediscovered and restored in the 19th and 20th centuries.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Borobudur's nine stacked platforms rising above Central Java jungle, stone stupas silhouetted against clouds

facts.quizQuestion: How many Buddha statues are there at Borobudur in Indonesia?
facts.explanation: Borobudur contains 504 Buddha statues distributed across its nine stacked platforms. The monument also has 2,672 relief panels and 72 perforated stupas on the upper circular terraces. It stands 35.4 metres high on a 123×123-metre base.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a row of stone Buddha statues in perforated stupas on Borobudur's upper terrace, misty volcano in the background

facts.quizQuestion: What is the total area of Angkor Wat, making it the world's largest religious complex?
facts.explanation: Angkor Wat covers 162.6 hectares, making it the world's largest religious complex. Built by Khmer king Suryavarman II between 1113 and 1150 CE, it was originally dedicated to the Hindu god Vishnu and later converted to Buddhism. It is the only major temple facing west.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Angkor Wat's five towers reflected in the grand reflecting pool at sunrise

facts.quizQuestion: Angkor Wat is unusual among Khmer temples in facing which cardinal direction?
facts.explanation: Angkor Wat faces west — unusual for a Khmer temple, as most face east. This western orientation is linked to Vishnu (associated with the west) and possibly to funerary symbolism, as the sun sets in the west. Its alignment also creates spectacular sunrise views behind the towers.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art aerial view of Angkor Wat oriented westward, the main entrance causeway stretching toward the setting sun

facts.quizQuestion: In which Indian city is the Golden Temple (Harmandir Sahib), the holiest shrine in Sikhism, located?
facts.explanation: The Golden Temple, officially known as Harmandir Sahib, is located in Amritsar, Punjab, India. The sacred pool (sarovar) was built by Guru Ram Das in 1577, and the gold leaf exterior was applied by Maharaja Ranjit Singh in 1830.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Golden Temple shimmering with gold leaf reflections on the Amrit Sarovar at dusk

facts.quizQuestion: Which Sikh ruler applied the famous gold leaf to the Golden Temple in 1830?
facts.explanation: Maharaja Ranjit Singh, founder of the Sikh Empire, had the upper floors of Harmandir Sahib covered in gold leaf in 1830 — giving the temple its common name 'Golden Temple.' The gold covering uses approximately 400 kg of pure gold.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Maharaja Ranjit Singh overseeing gold leaf craftsmen working on the temple's shimmering exterior

facts.quizQuestion: How many minarets does the Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed Mosque) in Istanbul have — a number that caused controversy when it was built?
facts.explanation: The Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed Mosque) has 6 minarets, which caused controversy when built in 1609–1617 because the Great Mosque of Mecca also had 6 minarets at the time, which was seen as inappropriate competition. A seventh minaret was added to Mecca to restore its supremacy.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Blue Mosque's six minarets rising at dusk, silhouetted against a violet Istanbul sky across from Hagia Sophia

facts.quizQuestion: How long did it take to build Cologne Cathedral in Germany — from 1248 to 1880?
facts.explanation: Cologne Cathedral took 632 years to build, from 1248 to 1880. When completed, it was briefly the world's tallest building (1880–1884) before being surpassed by the Washington Monument. It houses the Shrine of the Three Kings — a reliquary said to contain the remains of the Biblical Magi.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Cologne Cathedral's twin spires soaring above the Rhine, the Gothic masterpiece finally complete after six centuries

facts.quizQuestion: How tall are the twin spires of Cologne Cathedral?
facts.explanation: The twin spires of Cologne Cathedral reach 157 metres, making it the world's tallest twin-spired church. When completed in 1880, it was briefly the world's tallest structure (1880–1884) before being surpassed by the Washington Monument at 169 metres.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Cologne Cathedral's 157-metre twin spires piercing low clouds above the Rhine riverbank

facts.quizQuestion: Despite being called the 'Emerald Buddha,' what material is the famous statue at Wat Phra Kaew actually carved from?
facts.explanation: The Emerald Buddha at Wat Phra Kaew in Bangkok is actually carved from green jasper, not emerald. The statue is only 66 centimetres tall. Wat Phra Kaew is Thailand's most sacred Buddhist temple, located within the Grand Palace complex, and has no resident monks as it serves as a royal chapel.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the golden-robed Emerald Buddha sitting atop a towering throne in Wat Phra Kaew's ornate interior, gold and mirrored mosaic glittering

facts.quizQuestion: What is the total height of the Eiffel Tower in Paris, including its broadcast antenna?
facts.explanation: The Eiffel Tower stands 330 metres (1,083 ft) tall. Built by Gustave Eiffel's company for the 1889 World's Fair, it held the record as the world's tallest man-made structure for 41 years until the Chrysler Building surpassed it in 1930.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Eiffel Tower's iron lattice silhouette rising against a twilight Paris sky

facts.quizQuestion: What primary metal was used to construct the Eiffel Tower?
facts.explanation: The Eiffel Tower is built from puddled (wrought) iron — not steel. It uses approximately 7,300 tonnes of iron, with 18,000 individual parts bolted together by 2.5 million rivets. The tower is nicknamed 'La dame de fer' (The Iron Lady).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art close-up of riveted iron latticework in warm amber tones

facts.quizQuestion: For how many years did the Eiffel Tower hold the record as the world's tallest man-made structure (1889–1930)?
facts.explanation: The Eiffel Tower was the world's tallest structure from its completion in 1889 until 1930, when the Chrysler Building in New York City (319 m) surpassed it — a span of 41 years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art timeline showing the Eiffel Tower, then the Chrysler Building replacing it on a height chart

facts.quizQuestion: What is the total height of the Burj Khalifa in Dubai, the world's tallest building?
facts.explanation: The Burj Khalifa stands 829.8 metres (2,722 ft) tall, making it the world's tallest building. Completed in 2010 and designed by Skidmore, Owings & Merrill, it has 163 floors and is named after the UAE's founding president, Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Burj Khalifa's tapering spire piercing through golden desert haze above Dubai's skyline

facts.quizQuestion: How many floors does the Burj Khalifa have?
facts.explanation: The Burj Khalifa has 163 floors, more than any other building in the world. The building was constructed between 2004 and 2010 by Samsung C&T Corporation, with architecture by Skidmore, Owings & Merrill.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of the Burj Khalifa showing stacked floors narrowing to a needle point

facts.quizQuestion: How many days did it take to construct the Empire State Building from groundbreaking to completion?
facts.explanation: The Empire State Building was built in just 410 days — an astonishing feat of 1930s construction. Work began on March 17, 1930 and the building opened on May 1, 1931. At its peak, 3,400 workers laboured on site each day.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Art Deco Empire State Building gleaming in a 1930s New York cityscape

facts.quizQuestion: How many stories does the Empire State Building have?
facts.explanation: The Empire State Building has 102 stories and stands 443.2 metres (1,454 ft) tall including its antenna. It was the world's tallest building from 1931 until the North Tower of the World Trade Center surpassed it in 1970.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Empire State Building's distinctive stepped crown with Art Deco geometric patterns

facts.quizQuestion: What is the current lean angle of the Leaning Tower of Pisa after its 1993–2001 stabilisation project?
facts.explanation: The Leaning Tower of Pisa currently leans at 3.97° from vertical. Before the 1993–2001 stabilisation project, the lean was 5.5°. Engineers removed soil from the higher side to reduce the lean without straightening it completely — preserving its famous character.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the white marble Leaning Tower of Pisa against a blue Tuscan sky, tilt clearly visible

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did construction of the Leaning Tower of Pisa begin?
facts.explanation: Construction of the Leaning Tower of Pisa began in 1173. The tower is the bell tower (campanile) of Pisa Cathedral. It stands 55.86 metres on its high side, weighs approximately 14,500 tonnes, and has 296 steps to its top.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of medieval Pisan workers laying white marble blocks on the tower's first tier

facts.quizQuestion: What is the height of the CN Tower in Toronto, Canada?
facts.explanation: The CN Tower stands 553.3 metres (1,815 ft) tall. Built between 1973 and 1976, it held the record as the world's tallest free-standing structure for 32 years (1975–2007), when the Burj Dubai (now Burj Khalifa) surpassed it. CN stands for Canadian National Railway.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the CN Tower rising above Toronto's skyline with Lake Ontario shimmering in the background

facts.quizQuestion: For how many years (1975–2007) did the CN Tower hold the record as the world's tallest free-standing structure?
facts.explanation: The CN Tower held the record as the world's tallest free-standing structure from 1975 to 2007 — a span of 32 years. It was surpassed by the Burj Khalifa (then still under construction and called Burj Dubai) in 2007.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the CN Tower with a 32-year banner draped across its observation deck

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Tokyo Tower completed?
facts.explanation: Tokyo Tower was completed in 1958, designed by Nikken Sekkei. Standing 332.9 metres tall, it was inspired by the Eiffel Tower but painted in white and international orange for aviation safety. Remarkably, it used less steel than the Eiffel Tower despite being taller.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Tokyo Tower glowing orange and white against a 1958 Tokyo twilight sky

facts.quizQuestion: How much does the Big Ben bell weigh inside the Elizabeth Tower in London?
facts.explanation: 'Big Ben' is technically the name of the 13.5-tonne bell inside the tower, not the tower itself. The tower was known as the Clock Tower until 2012 when it was renamed the Elizabeth Tower to mark Queen Elizabeth II's Diamond Jubilee. The bell was cast in 1858.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Elizabeth Tower's ornate clock face at dusk with London's skyline behind it

facts.quizQuestion: How many steps lead to the top of the Elizabeth Tower (Big Ben) in London?
facts.explanation: The Elizabeth Tower has 334 steps to the top. The tower stands 96 metres tall and was completed in 1859. It was designed by Augustus Pugin, with the overall Palace of Westminster design by Charles Barry.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a spiral stone staircase winding upward inside the gothic Elizabeth Tower

facts.quizQuestion: What is the height of the Shanghai Tower, China's tallest building?
facts.explanation: The Shanghai Tower stands 632 metres (2,073 ft) tall with 128 stories, making it China's tallest building and the world's third tallest. Designed by Gensler and completed in 2015, it held the world's fastest elevators record at 20.5 m/s when it opened.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Shanghai Tower's spiral glass form twisting upward above the Pudong skyline

facts.quizQuestion: Which architect designed the Petronas Towers in Kuala Lumpur?
facts.explanation: The Petronas Towers were designed by Argentine-American architect César Pelli. Completed in 1998 at 451.9 metres with 88 floors each, they remain the world's tallest twin towers. Their floor plan is based on an Islamic eight-pointed star pattern.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the twin Petronas Towers rising above Kuala Lumpur with their distinctive Islamic star-pattern skybridge

facts.quizQuestion: How many floors does each of the Petronas Towers have?
facts.explanation: Each of the Petronas Towers has 88 floors, rising to 451.9 metres. They held the title of world's tallest buildings from 1998 to 2004. The number 88 is considered lucky in Chinese culture, significant in multicultural Malaysia.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art showing the identical 88-floor towers with their connecting sky bridge gleaming in equatorial sun

facts.quizQuestion: For which World's Fair was the Space Needle in Seattle built?
facts.explanation: The Space Needle was built for the 1962 World's Fair (Century 21 Exposition) in Seattle, Washington. Designed by John Graham Jr., it stands 184 metres tall. The tower is engineered to withstand magnitude 9.0 earthquakes.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Space Needle's flying-saucer observation deck hovering above 1960s Seattle against a Pacific Northwest sky

facts.quizQuestion: The Space Needle in Seattle is engineered to withstand an earthquake of what magnitude?
facts.explanation: The Space Needle is built to withstand a magnitude 9.0 earthquake. It is located in a seismically active region of the Pacific Northwest. Its foundation alone weighs nearly as much as the entire structure above ground.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Space Needle swaying slightly above seismic waves, remaining upright while buildings around it wobble

facts.quizQuestion: Which architecture firm designed the Burj Khalifa?
facts.explanation: The Burj Khalifa was designed by Skidmore, Owings & Merrill (SOM), with lead architect Adrian Smith. The firm also designed the Willis Tower (formerly Sears Tower) in Chicago and One World Trade Center in New York. The Burj Khalifa's Y-shaped floor plan was inspired by the Hymenocallis flower.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art showing the Burj Khalifa's Y-shaped floor plan overlaid on a blooming desert flower

facts.quizQuestion: Who designed and built the Eiffel Tower?
facts.explanation: The Eiffel Tower was designed and built by Gustave Eiffel's engineering company between 1887 and 1889. Eiffel was already famous for designing the internal iron framework of the Statue of Liberty before he built the tower.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art portrait of Gustave Eiffel in Victorian attire, standing beside blueprints of his tower

facts.quizQuestion: What architectural style is the Empire State Building built in?
facts.explanation: The Empire State Building is a classic example of Art Deco architecture, featuring the style's characteristic geometric ornamentation, stepped silhouette, and lavish use of chrome and stainless steel. It was designed by the firm Shreve, Lamb & Harmon and completed in 1931.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art showing the Empire State Building's Art Deco crown with geometric chrome detailing catching the morning sun

facts.quizQuestion: What unusual source provided some of the steel used to build Tokyo Tower, completed in 1958?
facts.explanation: Tokyo Tower was partly constructed using steel reclaimed from American tanks destroyed during the Korean War (1950–1953). The steel was melted down and repurposed into one of Japan's most peaceful and beloved peacetime landmarks — a remarkable transformation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art split panel: destroyed tanks on a Korean battlefield on the left, Tokyo Tower gleaming on the right

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how much does the Leaning Tower of Pisa weigh?
facts.explanation: The Leaning Tower of Pisa weighs approximately 14,500 tonnes. This immense weight concentrated on the soft subsoil of Pisa's alluvial plain is precisely what caused the lean — one side compressed more than the other.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art scale showing the Leaning Tower of Pisa on one side, balanced against 14,500 tonne weight blocks

facts.quizQuestion: How many stories does the Shanghai Tower have?
facts.explanation: The Shanghai Tower has 128 stories and stands 632 metres tall. Designed by Gensler and completed in 2015, it is China's tallest building. Its double-skin facade — an inner tower wrapped in an outer glass skin — reduces wind loads by 24% compared to a conventional skyscraper.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Shanghai Tower's spiralling glass exterior twisting from base to apex

facts.quizQuestion: What is the main span length of the Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco?
facts.explanation: The Golden Gate Bridge has a main span of 1,280 metres (4,200 ft). Opened in 1937 after four years of construction under chief engineer Joseph Strauss, it is recognised by the American Society of Civil Engineers as one of the Seven Wonders of the Modern World.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Golden Gate Bridge's vermilion towers rising through rolling San Francisco Bay fog

facts.quizQuestion: What is the official colour name of the paint used on the Golden Gate Bridge?
facts.explanation: The Golden Gate Bridge is painted 'International Orange' — a colour originally used as an anti-corrosion primer. Architect Irving Morrow insisted on it over the US Navy's preference for black-and-yellow stripes, and the public agreed it was iconic. Painting is essentially a continuous, never-ending process.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art paint bucket labelled 'International Orange' next to the Golden Gate Bridge silhouette

facts.quizQuestion: Who was the chief engineer of the Golden Gate Bridge?
facts.explanation: Joseph Strauss was the chief engineer of the Golden Gate Bridge. He championed the project for over a decade before construction began in 1933. The bridge opened on May 27, 1937 and was an ASCE Wonder of the Modern World.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Joseph Strauss in a hard hat standing at the construction site of the Golden Gate, blueprint in hand

facts.quizQuestion: What type of moveable bridge is London's Tower Bridge?
facts.explanation: Tower Bridge is a combined suspension and bascule bridge — the central section can be raised to allow tall ships through. It was originally powered by hydraulic steam engines; since 1976 the bascules are operated by electro-hydraulic drives. The bridge opens around 800 times per year.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Tower Bridge with its twin bascule sections raised, a tall ship passing beneath on the Thames

facts.quizQuestion: How long is London's Tower Bridge?
facts.explanation: Tower Bridge is 290 metres long with towers rising 65 metres above the Thames. Built between 1886 and 1894 to designs by Horace Jones and engineer John Wolfe Barry, it sees approximately 40,000 crossings daily.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Tower Bridge spanning the Thames with the Tower of London visible in the background

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the Hoover Dam on the Nevada–Arizona border?
facts.explanation: The Hoover Dam is 221.4 metres (726 ft) high and 379 metres (1,244 ft) long. A concrete arch-gravity dam, it was completed in 1936 — over two years ahead of schedule. It generates 2,078.8 MW of electricity and impounds Lake Mead.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Hoover Dam's massive curved concrete face holding back the turquoise waters of Lake Mead in the Nevada desert

facts.quizQuestion: What is the power generation capacity of the Hoover Dam?
facts.explanation: The Hoover Dam has a power generation capacity of 2,078.8 megawatts (MW), produced by 17 turbines. It supplies electricity to Nevada, Arizona, and California. Lake Mead, the reservoir it creates, is the largest reservoir in the United States by volume.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of electric current arcing from power lines anchored to the Hoover Dam, lighting up desert cities below

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the Panama Canal open to traffic?
facts.explanation: The Panama Canal opened on August 15, 1914. France attempted construction first in 1881 under Ferdinand de Lesseps but abandoned the project. The United States took over in 1904 and completed the 82-kilometre canal ten years later.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a 1914-era steamship entering the Panama Canal locks, with jungle-covered hillsides on both sides

facts.quizQuestion: How long is the Panama Canal?
facts.explanation: The Panama Canal is 82 kilometres (51 miles) long, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans through the Isthmus of Panama. It was expanded in 2016 to accommodate larger 'Neopanamax' ships with a new set of locks.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art map of the Panama Canal with ships traversing from ocean to ocean, locks visible as steps

facts.quizQuestion: What is the popular nickname of the Sydney Harbour Bridge?
facts.explanation: The Sydney Harbour Bridge is affectionately nicknamed 'The Coathanger' because of its distinctive single arch shape. Completed in 1932 with a main arch span of 504 metres, it is the tallest steel arch bridge in the world at 134 metres above sea level.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Sydney Harbour Bridge's steel arch against a golden Australian sunset with the Opera House visible beneath

facts.quizQuestion: How much steel was used in the construction of the Sydney Harbour Bridge?
facts.explanation: The Sydney Harbour Bridge used 52,800 tonnes of steel in its construction. Completed in 1932 after 9 years of construction, it was designed by engineer John Bradfield. Its arch span of 504 metres made it the longest steel arch bridge in the world at the time.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of steelworkers riveting the massive arch sections of the Sydney Harbour Bridge against a 1920s skyline

distractors: First fixed river crossing in America
facts.quizQuestion: What 'first' does the Brooklyn Bridge hold in engineering history?
facts.explanation: The Brooklyn Bridge was the world's first steel-wire suspension bridge when it opened in 1883. It also provided the first fixed crossing over the East River. The design was created by John A. Roebling, with construction completed under his son Washington Roebling and daughter-in-law Emily Warren Roebling.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Brooklyn Bridge's Gothic stone towers with steel suspension cables fanning outward over the East River

facts.quizQuestion: What is the main span length of the Brooklyn Bridge?
facts.explanation: The Brooklyn Bridge has a main span of 486.3 metres (1,595 ft). When it opened in 1883, it was the longest suspension bridge in the world. It connects the boroughs of Manhattan and Brooklyn over the East River in New York City.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Brooklyn Bridge with measurement annotations showing the 486-metre central span above the East River

facts.quizQuestion: What is the installed power generation capacity of the Three Gorges Dam — the world's largest power station?
facts.explanation: The Three Gorges Dam on China's Yangtze River has an installed capacity of 22,500 megawatts from 32 turbines (each rated at 700 MW), making it the world's largest power station. It was completed in 2006 and became fully operational in 2012.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Three Gorges Dam's enormous concrete face with 32 turbine housings and the Yangtze River below

facts.quizQuestion: How many people were displaced to build the Three Gorges Dam reservoir?
facts.explanation: Construction of the Three Gorges Dam displaced over 1.3 million people, flooding entire towns, farmland, and archaeological sites along the Yangtze River. The dam stands 185 metres high and 2,309 metres wide.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a submerged Chinese town beneath blue reservoir water, rooftops just visible below the surface

facts.quizQuestion: What is the main span length of Japan's Akashi Kaikyo Bridge — the world's second longest suspension span?
facts.explanation: The Akashi Kaikyo Bridge has a main span of 1,991 metres (6,532 ft), making it the second longest suspension bridge span in the world. Built between 1988 and 1998, it crosses the Akashi Strait in Japan.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Akashi Kaikyo Bridge's towers rising from the sea at twilight, cables stretching into the mist

facts.quizQuestion: Which major earthquake struck during construction of Japan's Akashi Kaikyo Bridge, shifting one tower by approximately 1 metre?
facts.explanation: The 1995 Great Hanshin (Kobe) earthquake, measuring 6.9 on the moment magnitude scale, struck during the bridge's construction and shifted one of its towers by approximately 1 metre. Engineers re-surveyed and redesigned to accommodate the shift, ultimately lengthening the main span by 1 metre.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art split: the 1995 Kobe earthquake's destruction on one side, the completed Akashi bridge above calm water on the other

facts.quizQuestion: Which British architect was responsible for the architectural design of France's Millau Viaduct?
facts.explanation: The Millau Viaduct was architecturally designed by Sir Norman Foster, with structural engineer Michel Virlogeux. Completed in 2004 at a structural height of 343 metres, it was the world's tallest bridge upon completion and is higher than the Eiffel Tower.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Millau Viaduct's slender pylons piercing through cloud layers above the Tarn River valley in France

facts.quizQuestion: What is the structural height of the Millau Viaduct in France?
facts.explanation: The Millau Viaduct reaches a structural height of 343 metres (1,125 ft), making it taller than the Eiffel Tower (330 m). It was the world's tallest bridge when it opened in 2004. The viaduct spans the Tarn River valley in southern France.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art height comparison: the Millau Viaduct's tallest pylon beside the Eiffel Tower, the pylon visibly taller

facts.quizQuestion: How long is the Suez Canal in Egypt?
facts.explanation: The Suez Canal is 193.3 kilometres (120 miles) long, connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea. Unlike the Panama Canal, it has no locks — the two seas are at the same level. It was opened in 1869 after construction led by Ferdinand de Lesseps.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art map of Egypt showing the Suez Canal as a blue line connecting the Mediterranean to the Red Sea, cargo ships queued

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the Suez Canal open?
facts.explanation: The Suez Canal opened in November 1869, after 10 years of construction led by French diplomat Ferdinand de Lesseps. It dramatically shortened the sailing route between Europe and Asia, eliminating the need to navigate around Africa's Cape of Good Hope.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an 1869 opening ceremony with sailing ships and steam vessels entering the newly finished canal, Egyptian desert on either side

facts.quizQuestion: Who led the construction of the Suez Canal?
facts.explanation: French diplomat Ferdinand de Lesseps led the construction of the Suez Canal, which opened in 1869. He later attempted to build the Panama Canal but failed. The 193.3-kilometre Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean and Red Seas with no locks.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art portrait of Ferdinand de Lesseps in 19th-century attire, standing before canal construction equipment in Egypt

facts.quizQuestion: By how much does the Sydney Harbour Bridge expand and contract due to temperature changes?
facts.explanation: The Sydney Harbour Bridge expands and contracts by up to 18 centimetres as temperatures rise and fall throughout the day. On very hot days the arch visibly rises relative to cooler conditions. Engineers monitor and account for this thermal movement in maintenance schedules.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art diagram showing the Sydney Harbour Bridge arch slightly higher on a hot summer day vs. a cool winter day

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the Three Gorges Dam on China's Yangtze River?
facts.explanation: The Three Gorges Dam stands 185 metres (607 ft) high and 2,309 metres (7,575 ft) wide. Completed in 2006, it is the world's largest power station. Its 32 main turbines each generate 700 MW, for a total installed capacity of 22,500 MW.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Three Gorges Dam's vast concrete face spanning the Yangtze gorge, water cascading through spillways

facts.quizQuestion: Who designed the Brooklyn Bridge?
facts.explanation: The Brooklyn Bridge was designed by civil engineer John A. Roebling. He died from tetanus in 1869, shortly after surveying the site, and never saw it built. Construction was completed by his son Washington Roebling and effectively managed by Washington's wife Emily Warren Roebling after Washington became incapacitated.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art portrait of John Roebling with the Brooklyn Bridge's Gothic towers rising behind him

facts.quizQuestion: How many locks does the Suez Canal have?
facts.explanation: The Suez Canal has no locks — unlike the Panama Canal, which has 6. The Mediterranean Sea and Red Sea are at essentially the same level, so ships can transit the canal without being raised or lowered. This makes it far simpler to operate than a lock-based canal.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art split comparison: the Panama Canal with stepped locks on the left, the flat Suez Canal with ships gliding through at sea level on the right

facts.quizQuestion: What is the arch span of the Sydney Harbour Bridge?
facts.explanation: The Sydney Harbour Bridge has an arch span of 504 metres (1,654 ft) and rises 134 metres above sea level. Completed in 1932 after nine years of construction, it was designed by engineer John Bradfield and remains the world's tallest steel arch bridge.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Sydney Harbour Bridge's two arch halves reaching toward each other from opposite shores before meeting

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the reservoir created by the Hoover Dam?
facts.explanation: The Hoover Dam impounds Lake Mead, the largest reservoir in the United States by volume. Straddling the Nevada–Arizona border, it stores water from the Colorado River and supplies water to roughly 25 million people across the US Southwest.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Lake Mead's turquoise water stretching into the distance behind the Hoover Dam, Nevada desert visible on both shores

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Louis XIV officially move the French royal court to the Palace of Versailles?
facts.explanation: Construction at Versailles began in 1661 under Louis XIV, but the king formally moved the royal court and government to the palace in 1682. The move consolidated power by bringing the entire French nobility under the king's watchful eye, reducing their ability to conspire.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a grand golden palace facade reflected in a vast rectangular fountain under a clear blue sky

facts.quizQuestion: The Treaty of Versailles, which formally ended World War I, was signed in what year at the Palace of Versailles?
facts.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919, in the Hall of Mirrors at the Palace of Versailles. The location was chosen deliberately — it was the same hall where the German Empire had been proclaimed in 1871 after the Franco-Prussian War, making the signing a pointed act of symbolic reversal.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an ornate gilded hall lined with enormous mirrors reflecting candlelight, diplomats gathered at a long table

facts.quizQuestion: Roughly how many buildings are contained within Beijing's Forbidden City complex?
facts.explanation: The Forbidden City contains approximately 980 surviving buildings spread across 72 hectares, with an estimated 8,886 rooms. It served as the imperial palace for 24 emperors across the Ming and Qing dynasties over more than 500 years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a sweeping aerial view of red-walled imperial buildings with golden curved rooftops stretching to the horizon

facts.quizQuestion: Which master builder is credited as the principal designer and construction supervisor of the Forbidden City, built between 1406 and 1420?
facts.explanation: Kuai Xiang served as the principal designer of the Forbidden City, which was constructed between 1406 and 1420 during the reign of the Yongle Emperor. The project employed roughly a million workers and brought in timber from Sichuan and stone from quarries near Beijing.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an ancient Chinese architect unrolling a scroll blueprint before a vast red-and-gold palace under construction

facts.quizQuestion: According to Guinness World Records, Neuschwanstein Castle in Bavaria is the world's tallest castle. Approximately how tall is it?
facts.explanation: Neuschwanstein Castle stands 65 metres (213 feet) tall and is recognized by Guinness World Records as the world's tallest castle. It was built starting in 1869 for King Ludwig II of Bavaria, who envisioned it as a personal retreat and tribute to the operas of Richard Wagner.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a white fairy-tale castle with pointed towers rising dramatically above pine forests and a misty mountain valley

facts.quizQuestion: Which famous Disney theme park castle was directly inspired by Neuschwanstein Castle in Bavaria?
facts.explanation: Walt Disney was inspired by Neuschwanstein Castle when designing Sleeping Beauty Castle at Disneyland in Anaheim, California, which opened in 1955. The fairy-tale silhouette of Neuschwanstein — with its steep towers and dramatic mountain setting — became the template for Disney's iconic castle imagery.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art split-panel showing Neuschwanstein's white turrets on the left and a colourful cartoon version of a Disney castle on the right

facts.quizQuestion: Which Islamic dynasty built the Alhambra palace complex in Granada, Spain, beginning in 1238?
facts.explanation: The Alhambra was built beginning in 1238 by the Nasrid dynasty, the last Muslim rulers of the Iberian Peninsula. It is considered the best-preserved medieval Islamic palace in the world, renowned for its intricate geometric tilework and carved stucco decorations.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an arched Islamic courtyard with intricate geometric tile patterns, a central fountain, and slender columns

facts.quizQuestion: In 1492, where did Christopher Columbus receive royal endorsement from Ferdinand and Isabella for his voyage to the Americas?
facts.explanation: Columbus received his royal endorsement at the Alhambra palace in Granada in January 1492 — the same year Ferdinand and Isabella completed the Reconquista by conquering the last Nasrid sultanate. The convergence of these events at one place makes the Alhambra a pivotal site in world history.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Columbus presenting plans to a king and queen in a Moorish palace with horseshoe arches and starry tilework

facts.quizQuestion: Who founded the Tower of London in 1066 and began construction of the White Tower around 1078?
facts.explanation: William the Conqueror established the Tower of London after his victory at the Battle of Hastings in 1066. He built the White Tower around 1078 as a symbol of Norman power. Over the centuries the complex grew to cover 12 acres and served as a royal residence, armory, treasury, mint, and prison.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a stern Norman knight before a grey stone fortress tower with a Union Jack banner, ravens perched on the battlements

facts.quizQuestion: At approximately what altitude above sea level does the Potala Palace sit in Lhasa, Tibet?
facts.explanation: The Potala Palace sits at an altitude of approximately 3,700 metres (12,100 feet) above sea level in Lhasa, Tibet. The palace itself rises 119 metres above Marpo Ri hill and more than 300 metres above the valley floor. It served as the Dalai Lama's winter palace until 1959 and is now a museum.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a vast white-and-red palace rising tier by tier up a rocky hill against a vivid Himalayan sky

facts.quizQuestion: How many rooms does Buckingham Palace have?
facts.explanation: Buckingham Palace has 775 rooms in total, including 19 State rooms, 52 royal and guest bedrooms, 188 staff bedrooms, 92 offices, and 78 bathrooms. The core structure dates to 1703, but it became the official royal residence in 1837 under Queen Victoria.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a grand cream-coloured palace facade with the Royal Standard flying above the gates as a crowd gathers below

facts.quizQuestion: Windsor Castle, the oldest and largest inhabited castle in the world, was originally founded by which Norman king in the 11th century?
facts.explanation: Windsor Castle was founded by William the Conqueror in the 11th century as a royal fortress. Spanning 13 acres, it is the oldest and largest occupied castle in the world and has been home to more than 40 British monarchs across over 900 years of continuous royal use.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive grey stone castle with round towers and a flag on the keep, set on a broad green hill

facts.quizQuestion: The Spoonmaker's Diamond on display at Topkapi Palace in Istanbul weighs how many carats?
facts.explanation: The Spoonmaker's Diamond (Kaşıkçı Elması) at Topkapi Palace weighs 86 carats and is one of the largest diamonds on public display in the world. Topkapi Palace was built by Mehmed the Conqueror starting in 1459 and served as the administrative centre of the Ottoman Empire for nearly 400 years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive teardrop diamond glittering in an ornate display case inside a domed Ottoman palace treasury

facts.quizQuestion: Which Ottoman sultan ordered the construction of Topkapi Palace in Istanbul, beginning in 1459?
facts.explanation: Topkapi Palace was built on the orders of Mehmed the Conqueror (Mehmed II) beginning in 1459, six years after his conquest of Constantinople. The palace covered between 59,260 and 70,000 square metres and served as the seat of Ottoman power for nearly 400 years, until Sultan Abdulmecid I moved to Dolmabahce Palace in 1853.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a stern Ottoman sultan on horseback surveying a sprawling palace complex overlooking a sparkling blue strait

facts.quizQuestion: What is the popular nickname of Himeji Castle in Japan, given for its gleaming white plastered exterior?
facts.explanation: Himeji Castle is nicknamed 'White Heron Castle' (Shirasagi-jo or Hakuro-jo) because its brilliant white plaster exterior and graceful silhouette resemble a white heron in flight. The main keep, completed in 1609, stands 46.4 metres tall and is part of a complex of 83 buildings.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a gleaming white multi-tiered Japanese castle with sweeping curved rooftops rising above pink cherry blossoms

facts.quizQuestion: Construction of Himeji Castle's famous main keep began in what year?
facts.explanation: Although a fortress first stood on the site from 1333, the main keep of Himeji Castle was constructed between 1601 and 1609 under the direction of Ikeda Terumasa. The castle was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1993 and is one of only twelve original castles surviving in Japan.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a white tiered Japanese castle keep seen from a narrow stone path flanked by thick walls, under an autumn sky

facts.quizQuestion: How many windows does the Palace of Versailles have?
facts.explanation: The Palace of Versailles has 2,143 windows, along with 700 rooms and 1,252 chimneys. The palace was originally a hunting lodge for Louis XIII before Louis XIV transformed it into Europe's greatest royal palace beginning in 1661, using architects Louis Le Vau and Jules Hardouin-Mansart.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art showing a long golden palace facade, hundreds of arched windows glinting in the afternoon sunlight above formal French gardens

facts.quizQuestion: How many emperors of China lived in the Forbidden City across the Ming and Qing dynasties?
facts.explanation: A total of 24 emperors — 14 from the Ming dynasty and 10 from the Qing dynasty — lived in the Forbidden City over more than 500 years. The last emperor, Puyi, was expelled from the palace in 1924, and it became the Palace Museum in 1925.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of 24 imperial portrait plaques arranged in a grid, each showing a robed Chinese emperor against a golden backdrop

facts.quizQuestion: What famous collection of royal regalia is kept and displayed at the Tower of London?
facts.explanation: The British Crown Jewels — including the Imperial State Crown and the Sovereign's Sceptre — are kept in the Jewel House at the Tower of London. The collection includes over 100 objects and dates back centuries. The Tower has served as a royal residence, armoury, treasury, mint, and prison since its founding in 1066.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a glittering crown and sceptre displayed behind thick glass in a vaulted stone chamber guarded by a Yeoman Warder

facts.quizQuestion: Which Dalai Lama ordered construction of the modern Potala Palace in Lhasa in 1649?
facts.explanation: The modern Potala Palace was built beginning in 1649 on the orders of the 5th Dalai Lama (Ngawang Lobsang Gyatso). It replaced an earlier structure and became the winter residence of Dalai Lamas. The 14th Dalai Lama used it as his winter palace until fleeing to India in 1959; it is now a museum and UNESCO World Heritage Site.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a robed Buddhist monk looking out from a high balcony of a great white-and-red palace, mountains rising behind

facts.quizQuestion: Which flag flies above Buckingham Palace to signal that the monarch is present in the building?
facts.explanation: The Royal Standard flies above Buckingham Palace whenever the reigning monarch is present. When the monarch is away, the Union Flag (commonly called the Union Jack) is flown instead. This tradition dates back centuries and allows the public to know at a glance whether the sovereign is at home.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Buckingham Palace's gate and balcony with a bold heraldic flag flying high above a cheering crowd below

facts.quizQuestion: Washington Irving's celebrated book 'Tales of the Alhambra', written after he lived in the palace, was published in what year?
facts.explanation: Washington Irving published 'Tales of the Alhambra' in 1832 after residing in the palace's then-empty apartments. His romantic accounts of Moorish legends and the faded grandeur of the palace sparked enormous international interest in the Alhambra and contributed to efforts to restore and preserve it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a man writing by candlelight in a Moorish arched room, a quill in hand, moonlight streaming through latticed windows

facts.quizQuestion: Which daimyo commissioned and supervised the construction of Himeji Castle's main keep between 1601 and 1609?
facts.explanation: Ikeda Terumasa, a powerful daimyo and son-in-law of Tokugawa Ieyasu, received the Harima domain as a reward after the Battle of Sekigahara in 1600 and immediately began expanding the castle at Himeji. The result — completed in 1609 — is considered the finest surviving example of Japanese castle architecture.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a samurai lord in formal court dress presenting a rolled architectural plan to a group of builders before a rising white castle

facts.quizQuestion: King Ludwig II of Bavaria lived in Neuschwanstein Castle for how many days in total before his mysterious death in 1886?
facts.explanation: Despite commissioning Neuschwanstein Castle as his personal retreat in 1869, Ludwig II only lived there for 172 days before his death in 1886. The castle was still unfinished when he died. He was declared mentally unfit and deposed just days before his death — he was found dead in a shallow lake the next day under unexplained circumstances.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a lone king in royal robes standing at a turret window of an unfinished fairytale castle, looking pensively into misty mountains

facts.quizQuestion: Windsor Castle covers approximately how many acres, making it the largest occupied castle in the world?
facts.explanation: Windsor Castle covers approximately 13 acres (5.26 hectares), making it the largest occupied castle in the world. It has been the home of English and British monarchs for over 900 years. The current monarch uses Windsor Castle as a weekend retreat and for various state occasions.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an aerial view showing a sprawling grey stone castle complex with circular towers set on a grassy hill above a riverside town

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Topkapi Palace in Istanbul converted into a museum, one year after the founding of the Turkish Republic?
facts.explanation: Topkapi Palace became a public museum in 1924, the year after Mustafa Kemal Atatürk founded the Republic of Turkey. The Ottoman sultans had already moved their primary residence to Dolmabahce Palace in 1853. The museum today holds one of the world's great collections of Ottoman art, Chinese porcelain, and Islamic relics.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an ornate domed Ottoman palace gate hung with a museum sign, tourists streaming through beneath a Turkish flag

facts.quizQuestion: Who sculpted the Statue of Liberty, dedicated in New York Harbor in 1886?
facts.explanation: The Statue of Liberty was sculpted by French artist Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi. Gustave Eiffel (of Eiffel Tower fame) engineered the statue's iron internal framework, while architect Richard Morris Hunt designed the pedestal. The statue was a gift from France to the United States, officially named 'Liberty Enlightening the World'.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Statue of Liberty in her original copper-brown colour beside a modern version in verdigris green, a boat in the harbour below

facts.quizQuestion: Including its pedestal, what is the total height of the Statue of Liberty from ground to torch?
facts.explanation: The Statue of Liberty stands 46.1 metres (151 feet) tall on its own, but including the pedestal the total height from ground to torch tip is 93.1 metres (305 feet). The statue was dedicated on October 28, 1886, and was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1984.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a scale comparison showing the Statue of Liberty next to a skyscraper, with a measurement ruler from the ground to the torch tip

facts.quizQuestion: Christ the Redeemer statue in Rio de Janeiro is built in which architectural and artistic style?
facts.explanation: Christ the Redeemer is built in the Art Deco style, constructed of reinforced concrete and covered in soapstone tiles. It was sculpted by French-Polish artist Paul Landowski and engineered by Brazilian Heitor da Silva Costa. The statue was completed in 1931 and stands on Corcovado mountain at 710 metres above Rio de Janeiro.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the outstretched arms of a white stone Art Deco Christ statue, lightning bolts crackling in a stormy sky behind it

facts.quizQuestion: What is the total height of Christ the Redeemer including its pedestal?
facts.explanation: Christ the Redeemer stands 30 metres tall with an additional 8-metre pedestal, giving a total height of 38 metres. It sits atop Corcovado mountain at 710 metres above sea level, making it visible from much of Rio de Janeiro. It was voted one of the New Seven Wonders of the World in 2007.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Christ the Redeemer lit in golden light against a sunset sky, the city of Rio and the bay glittering far below

facts.quizQuestion: Which sculptor directed the carving of Mount Rushmore in South Dakota, beginning in 1927?
facts.explanation: Gutzon Borglum directed the carving of Mount Rushmore from 1927 until his death in 1941, months before the project was completed. His son Lincoln Borglum finished the project. The 60-foot faces of Washington, Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt, and Lincoln were carved by removing 450,000 tons of rock, primarily using dynamite.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of four massive presidential faces emerging from grey granite cliffs, pine trees stretching across the Black Hills below

facts.quizQuestion: Which four US presidents are depicted at Mount Rushmore?
facts.explanation: Mount Rushmore depicts George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt, and Abraham Lincoln, chosen by sculptor Gutzon Borglum to represent America's founding, expansion, conservation, and preservation of the Union respectively. The faces are each approximately 60 feet (18 metres) tall.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of four grey stone presidential faces labelled with names, carved into a cliff face under an American flag

facts.quizQuestion: How many Doric columns does the Lincoln Memorial have, and what do they represent?
facts.explanation: The Lincoln Memorial has 36 Doric columns, one for each state in the Union at the time of Lincoln's death in 1865. The memorial was designed by architect Henry Bacon and dedicated in 1922. The statue of Lincoln inside, 5.8 metres tall, was sculpted by Daniel Chester French.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a white neoclassical temple with 36 tall columns, a massive seated stone president visible through the entrance

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Martin Luther King Jr. deliver his 'I Have a Dream' speech from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial?
facts.explanation: Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his famous 'I Have a Dream' speech on August 28, 1963, from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial during the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom. An estimated 250,000 people attended the rally on the National Mall, making it one of the largest political demonstrations in American history.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a man at a podium on white marble steps addressing a vast crowd stretching down the National Mall toward the Washington Monument

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the Arc de Triomphe in Paris?
facts.explanation: The Arc de Triomphe stands 49.54 metres (162.5 feet) tall. Commissioned by Napoleon Bonaparte in 1806 following his victory at Austerlitz, it was completed in 1836 — after Napoleon's death. Below it lies the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, with an eternal flame that has burned since 1923.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive stone triumphal arch at the centre of a busy roundabout at night, an eternal flame glowing at its base

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Napoleon Bonaparte commission the Arc de Triomphe in Paris?
facts.explanation: Napoleon commissioned the Arc de Triomphe in 1806 after his decisive victory at the Battle of Austerlitz the previous year. Architect Jean-François Chalgrin designed it. Napoleon died in 1821 and the arch was not completed until 1836 under King Louis-Philippe. Napoleon's ashes passed under the arch in 1840 during their transfer to Les Invalides.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Napoleon on horseback saluting a canvas-and-timber mockup arch while the real stone foundations are being laid in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many moai statues were carved by the Rapa Nui people on Easter Island?
facts.explanation: More than 900 moai statues were carved by the Rapa Nui people between approximately 1250 and 1500 CE. They were carved from volcanic tuff quarried at Rano Raraku and represent deified ancestors. The tallest erected moai, known as Paro, stands about 10 metres tall and weighs 82 tonnes. Easter Island is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1995).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a row of giant stone heads with heavy brows and long ears standing on a grassy hillside above the Pacific Ocean

facts.quizQuestion: What material were the moai statues of Easter Island primarily carved from?
facts.explanation: The moai were carved primarily from volcanic tuff — a type of compressed volcanic ash — quarried at the Rano Raraku volcanic crater on Easter Island. Roughly 400 unfinished moai remain at the quarry, frozen in various stages of completion. A smaller number of moai were carved from basalt, coral, or red scoria.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of stone carvers at work inside a volcanic crater, chipping away at a half-formed giant face in the grey tuff rock

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the Gateway of India in Mumbai?
facts.explanation: The Gateway of India stands 26 metres (85 feet) tall and was built to commemorate the visit of King George V and Queen Mary to India in 1911. Designed by architect George Wittet in the Indo-Saracenic style, it was constructed between 1913 and 1924 in Mumbai Harbour.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a tall yellow basalt archway on a harbour waterfront, small boats in the bay and the Taj Mahal Hotel visible behind it

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the last British troops march through the Gateway of India while departing the country?
facts.explanation: The last British troops to leave India marched through the Gateway of India in 1948, after Indian independence in 1947. The Somerset Light Infantry was the last regiment to leave, boarding ships in Mumbai Harbour in February 1948. The event was deeply symbolic — the monument built to celebrate British imperial arrival had become the exit point for the retreating empire.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of uniformed soldiers in colonial dress marching through a monumental arch toward waiting ships on a sun-lit harbour

facts.quizQuestion: Which architect designed the Brandenburg Gate in Berlin, completed in 1791?
facts.explanation: The Brandenburg Gate was designed by Carl Gotthard Langhans and built between 1788 and 1791. It is 26 metres tall and 65.5 metres wide, modelled after the Propylaea gateway to the Acropolis in Athens. It is the only surviving city gate of the original 14 gates of Berlin.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a neoclassical stone gate with six Doric columns and a bronze four-horse chariot on top, the Berlin skyline visible beyond

facts.quizQuestion: The Brandenburg Gate became a symbol of what major historical event in 1989?
facts.explanation: The Brandenburg Gate became a powerful symbol of German reunification in 1989 when the Berlin Wall fell. For 28 years (1961-1989) the gate had stood in the closed border zone between East and West Berlin, inaccessible to citizens of either side. When the Wall opened on November 9, 1989, crowds gathered at the gate in one of the 20th century's most celebrated moments.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of jubilant crowds climbing atop a broken concrete wall before the Brandenburg Gate, lit up in celebration at night

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the Washington Monument — the world's tallest obelisk — in Washington DC?
facts.explanation: The Washington Monument stands 169.046 metres (554 feet 7 inches) tall, making it the world's tallest stone obelisk and tallest monumental column. Construction began in 1848 and was completed in 1884 after a 25-year pause. From 1884 to 1889 it was the tallest structure in the world.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a towering white stone obelisk reflected in a long rectangular pool, the US Capitol visible in the distant background

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did construction of the Washington Monument halt — creating the visible two-tone colour difference on the obelisk today?
facts.explanation: Construction of the Washington Monument halted in 1854 due to lack of funds and controversy over management of the project, and resumed only in 1879. The 25-year gap caused the visible two-tone band on the obelisk — marble blocks quarried after the pause came from a different section of the quarry and are slightly different in colour.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Washington Monument showing a subtle colour change partway up the obelisk, with a close-up inset showing the band clearly

facts.quizQuestion: Which Mughal emperor built the Taj Mahal in Agra, India, as a mausoleum for his wife?
facts.explanation: The Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal between 1631 and 1653 as a mausoleum for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth in 1631. The complex covers 17 hectares and employed approximately 20,000 artisans. It was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Taj Mahal's white marble dome reflected perfectly in a long rectangular pool at sunrise, framed by cypress trees

facts.quizQuestion: What primary material gives the Taj Mahal its brilliant white appearance?
facts.explanation: The Taj Mahal is built primarily from white Makrana marble sourced from Rajasthan. The marble is semi-translucent and changes colour subtly depending on the time of day and light conditions — appearing pinkish at dawn, dazzling white at noon, and golden under moonlight. The main dome is 73 metres tall.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a close-up of the Taj Mahal's white marble wall showing delicate inlaid flower patterns in coloured gemstones

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many artisans worked to build the Taj Mahal in Agra?
facts.explanation: Approximately 20,000 artisans were employed to build the Taj Mahal between 1631 and 1653. They came from across the Mughal Empire and beyond — including craftsmen from Persia, the Ottoman Empire, and Central Asia. The principal architect was Ustad Ahmad Lahori.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of hundreds of artisans in turbans working on the Taj Mahal's scaffolding — some carving marble, others inlaying gemstones

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is Paro, the tallest erected moai statue on Easter Island, which weighs approximately 82 tonnes?
facts.explanation: Paro, the tallest erected moai on Easter Island, stands approximately 10 metres (33 feet) tall and weighs approximately 82 tonnes. It stands on the Ahu Te Pito Kura platform on the north coast. Most moai are between 3.5 and 5.5 metres tall; Paro is exceptional in both height and weight.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive stone moai head towering above a person standing beside it for scale, the Pacific Ocean visible in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Who was the French-Polish sculptor who created the figure of Christ the Redeemer in Rio de Janeiro?
facts.explanation: Christ the Redeemer was sculpted by French-Polish artist Paul Landowski, who modelled the figure in clay sections in his Paris studio. Brazilian engineer Heitor da Silva Costa designed the overall structure and supervised construction on Corcovado mountain between 1922 and 1931.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a sculptor in a Paris studio working on large clay arm sections, with the Corcovado mountain blueprint pinned on the wall behind

facts.quizQuestion: The Grand Canyon stretches along the Colorado River — how long is it?
facts.explanation: The Grand Canyon in Arizona, USA, is 277 miles (446 km) long, up to 18 miles wide, and reaches a maximum depth of 1,857 meters. The Colorado River carved it over 5–6 million years, exposing nearly 2 billion years of Earth's geological history.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art aerial view of a vast red-and-orange canyon with a winding blue river at the bottom under a bright desert sky

facts.quizQuestion: Which Spanish explorer became the first European to see the Grand Canyon in 1540?
facts.explanation: García López de Cárdenas, a lieutenant in Francisco Vásquez de Coronado's expedition, reached the rim of the Grand Canyon in 1540. He was guided there by Hopi people and spent three days trying unsuccessfully to descend to the Colorado River below.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Spanish conquistador in armor standing at a canyon rim, gazing into a vast red gorge

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the Grand Canyon become a US National Park?
facts.explanation: President Woodrow Wilson signed the Grand Canyon National Park Act on February 26, 1919, establishing it as the 17th US National Park. Theodore Roosevelt had declared it a National Monument in 1908, protecting it years before it received full National Park status.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a vintage National Park sign in front of a sweeping canyon panorama under a golden sunset

facts.quizQuestion: Stretching along the Queensland coast, approximately how long is the Great Barrier Reef?
facts.explanation: The Great Barrier Reef stretches approximately 2,300 km along the northeastern coast of Queensland, Australia. It comprises over 2,900 individual reefs and 900 islands, covering an area of 344,400 km². It became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1981.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a vibrant coral reef underwater with hundreds of colorful fish, viewed from above showing Queensland's coastline

facts.quizQuestion: The Great Barrier Reef is not one reef but a system of how many individual reefs?
facts.explanation: The Great Barrier Reef is composed of over 2,900 individual reefs plus more than 900 islands. It covers an area of 344,400 km², making it the world's largest coral reef system and the largest structure made by living organisms on Earth.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art top-down map view of the Queensland coast showing thousands of tiny reef patches in shades of turquoise and blue

facts.quizQuestion: What is the local Kololo-language name for Victoria Falls, meaning 'The Smoke That Thunders'?
facts.explanation: Victoria Falls is known as 'Mosi-oa-Tunya' in the Kololo language, meaning 'The Smoke That Thunders.' The spray from the falls — which is 1,708 meters wide and 108 meters tall — can be seen from up to 50 km away, looking like rising smoke.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive waterfall sending a column of white mist high into the sky, visible across a flat African savanna

facts.quizQuestion: Which explorer named Victoria Falls in 1855, after the British queen?
facts.explanation: Scottish explorer David Livingstone became the first European to see Victoria Falls on November 16, 1855, and named it after Queen Victoria. He described it as 'scenes so lovely must have been gazed upon by angels in their flight.' The falls sit on the Zambia-Zimbabwe border.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Victorian-era explorer in a pith helmet standing at a canyon edge overlooking a thundering waterfall

facts.quizQuestion: Victoria Falls on the Zambia-Zimbabwe border is the world's largest curtain of falling water — how wide is it?
facts.explanation: Victoria Falls is 1,708 meters wide and 108 meters tall. Its average flow is 1,088 cubic meters per second. This combination of width and height makes it the world's largest sheet of falling water — larger than Niagara Falls by volume.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art wide-angle view of a mile-wide waterfall plunging into a gorge, with a rainbow forming in the permanent mist cloud

facts.quizQuestion: Per the 2020 joint China-Nepal survey, how tall is Mount Everest?
facts.explanation: The 2020 joint China-Nepal survey established Mount Everest's official height at 8,848.86 meters (29,031.7 ft) above sea level. This replaced the previous figure of 8,848 m set in 1954. Everest sits on the China-Nepal border and grows approximately 4 mm per year from tectonic uplift.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a snow-capped pyramid peak towering above surrounding mountains, with the Tibetan plateau stretching behind it

facts.quizQuestion: When did Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay first reach the summit of Mount Everest?
facts.explanation: On May 29, 1953, New Zealander Edmund Hillary and Nepali-Indian Sherpa Tenzing Norgay became the first people confirmed to have reached the summit of Mount Everest. They were part of a British expedition led by John Hunt. The news reached London just in time for Queen Elizabeth II's coronation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two climbers in heavy winter gear planting a flag on a snowy peak at sunrise, surrounded by clouds far below

facts.quizQuestion: What is the Nepali name for Mount Everest, meaning 'Goddess of the Sky'?
